body.p:
DITA Wikipedia page on the DITA standard DITA is such a standard for technical documentation. General parts can be processed by all DITA systems - that is, all parts of the information that are to be made available to the outside world, for example, supplier documentation for specific machine parts.
UWE test footnote (11/40, callout K): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.

Specific information, such as references to an internal database, would be either swallowed or otherwise flagged by an external DITA processor - depending on how the processing of unknown elements is implemented in the external system.

section.title: DITA vs Docbook
section.p: Roughly speaking, the DITA structure resembles that of Docbook DocBook. It has the typical chapter nesting.
For comparison, a Docbook skeleton and a DITA skeleton (Wikipedia) are shown below.
section.pre: <book id="einfaches_buch" <title>Ein sehr einfaches Buch</title> <chapter id="einfaches_kapitel"> <title>Kapitel</title> <para>Hallo Welt!</para> </chapter> </book>

Maintaining You maintain your solution to ensure that all components are operating at maximum efficiency.

Maintenance is a task that you perform along with configuration to get the most from your solution.
section.p: While the Docbook standard still has a <book> element wrapping the individual chapters, the approach in the DITA standard is topic-based - keyword: Topic Based AuthoringBuzzwordsTopic Based Authoring .

Here, the editor no longer focuses as much on the book as a whole, but more on the self-containedness and referenceability of the individual topics (= information units), in order to easily reuse them in another publication, possibly in a different format (online, print, smartphone).

In the XML snippet above, one might guess, for example, that the <shortdesc> element could be displayed on an overview page in an electronic publication (to characterize the reference to the actual topic), while in a print publication this information could be printed as an introductory short text.
But what is the trick with the DITA standard? Document Type Definitions (DTDs) that rename some elements and provide a few semantic specialties are plentiful.

section.title: The Trick with DITA
section.p: I think what is special about DITA are a few technical tricks that realize a certain genericity of the approach. If we also include the DTD in the XML snippet above:
and run an identity transformation, additional attributes have magically appeared, cf.
section.pre:
Maintaining You maintain your solution to ensure that all components are operating at maximum efficiency.

Maintenance is a task that you perform along with configuration to get the most from your solution.
section.p: Particularly interesting here is the @class attribute. How did this happen?

note.p: DITA defines a number of #FIXED attributes selfhtml.org (attributes) with a fixed value that the parser must automatically attach to the elements during validation. Unparsed, a topic looks quite simple. Parsed, the DITA XML can appear quite bloated.

section.p: XSLT rules in a DITA processor do not match on element names but on the @class attributes, e.g. like this:
Here, the title of the topic is to be formatted. In a specialization DITA specialization (IBM) of the topic - that would be, for example, a Concept:
section.pre:
My concept Just a concept to illustrate specialization

Yeah, we need some content here, too!
section.p: the #FIXED attributes are now set as follows:
section.pre:
My concept Just a concept to illustrate specialization

Yeah, we need some content here, too!
section.p: that formats a title, would therefore apply to both topic elements and concept elements. If you want a special formatting for concept elements, you would need to add the following rule to the rule base:

Why is all this so clever? If, for example, company XYZ has not yet created concept definitions, it can still process concept elements from company ABC, because the match rules do not operate on element names but on class attributes dynamically added by the DTD - which contain not only the identifiers of the specialization but also the identifiers of the generalization. Every DITA processor knows these and can at least provide a default.

For the above example - regarding the title - this would mean that the concept title in company XYZ would not be displayed as nicely (or at a specific position) as intended in company ABC. However, the important content is still displayed.

note.p: The reference implementation for DITA is, by the way, the DITA Open Toolkit DITA Open Toolkit

section.p: Creating a low-redundancy DITA DTD is, however, a science in itself. After all, you want to maintain the generic approach for your own specializations as well. The DITA editor - or rather the documentation manager - must therefore adhere to a fairly complex naming and structural convention.
